title: tmuxcc shared wire primitives
description: Shared identifier types and envelope primitives used by both the server-proxy wire and the session-proxy wire.

PaneId.description: Opaque pane identifier minted by the session-proxy (e.g. "p0", "p3"). Never exposes tmux's internal %N pane numbers.

WindowId.description: Opaque window identifier minted by the session-proxy (e.g. "w0", "w3"). Never exposes tmux window indices.

SessionId.description: Opaque session identifier minted by the server-proxy (e.g. "s0", "s2"). Never exposes tmux session names or numeric IDs directly.

ConnectionId.description: Opaque per-connection identifier minted by the session-proxy when a client connects (e.g. "conn1", "conn2"). Stable for the life of one client connection; reused from a slot pool after disconnect (bounded by max concurrent clients). Used to attribute the origin of a verb-caused creation delta (tc-ozk.2): a `pane.opened` / `window.added` carries `origin.connectionId` naming the connection whose wire verb caused it. A client compares against its OWN connectionId (advertised in the snapshot) to decide whether a creation is its own.

Origin.description: Causality tag on a creation delta (tc-ozk.2). PRESENT means the delta was caused by a wire verb (split-pane / open-window / break-pane) from `connectionId`, whose `command.request` carried `requestId` as its correlationId. ABSENT (the field is omitted) means the creation is FOREIGN — a native tmux client, a script, or any non-wire cause. The session-proxy is the only party that knows who caused what (it correlates the verb's returned effect ids, tc-ozk.1, to the creation it emits), so it stamps this; clients never guess.

requestId.description: The correlationId of the originating `command.request` (echoed verbatim). Lets the causing client match the creation to the specific verb it issued.

MessageBase.description: Every control-plane message on both wires carries a `type` discriminant and a per-connection per-sender monotonically-increasing sequence number starting at 1.

seq.description: Per-connection per-sender sequence number, starting at 1, incremented by the sender for each message. Counters are independent between server-proxy and session-proxy connections even when both originate from the same client.

WireFeature.description: Named feature flag. Unknown strings MUST be silently ignored by older implementations (forward-compatible). Valid values are split between the server-proxy wire and the session-proxy wire.

Capabilities.description: Feature flags and version info exchanged during handshake. Each side advertises its own capabilities; the effective feature set is the intersection.

protocolVersion.description: Wire protocol version this endpoint implements. Must equal WIRE_PROTOCOL_VERSION (currently 3). Handshake fails if both sides advertise different values.

features.description: Feature flags this endpoint supports. Both sides advertise; the intersection is the effective feature set.

ClientIdentity.description: Durable identity a client presents at handshake (D2, tc-4b6k.1). The enabler for every per-client fact (binding intent D3, per-client size D4): today the wire has no notion of WHICH client is connected, so a per-client fact has no axis to hang on. The identity is presented on `client.capabilities` (both wires) and is otherwise inert in this protocol revision — carried and logged only, no behavior change. `id` is the load-bearing key downstream beads (D3/D4) use to store/serve per-(object, client) facts; the driver treats it as opaque.

id.description: Durable client identity string, STABLE ACROSS the client's own reloads (a VS Code window reload presents the same id). Opaque to the driver. The VS Code client derives it from the workspace (extension helpers, §7.2 workspace-derived identity), but the derivation is a client-side detail: the wire deliberately does not encode host vocabulary (wire invariant: no renderer/host types). Two distinct durable clients MUST present distinct ids; the same durable client MUST present the same id across reconnects.

label.description: Optional human-readable label for logs and diagnostics (e.g. a workspace basename). NEVER load-bearing — the driver uses `id` for all per-client keying; `label` is display-only and may be absent, empty, or non-unique.

ClientFlags.description: Per-client tmux-parity attach flags (D4/D8, decisions §2.1). RESERVED-NOT-IMPLEMENTED in tc-4b6k.1: this $def defines the protocol SLOTS the two minimum flags will occupy on `session.attach` — the attach wire step is created by tc-4b6k.4, and the driver behavior (owner-drives-size / observer partition) is tc-4b6k.3. Nothing carries this object on the wire yet; it exists so later beads land on a defined shape, and conformance validates the shape directly. The vocabulary mirrors tmux(1) client flags; the wider parity map (per-client size, active-pane, pause-after, no-detach-on-destroy, session groups) is enumerated in PROTOCOL.md as reserved prose, not typed here.

ignoreSize.description: tmux-parity `ignore-size` flag: this client does not contribute its viewport to the session's size arbitration (only the owning client drives `refresh-client -C`). Reserved slot; the driver does not act on it in tc-4b6k.1 (owner-only size authority is tc-4b6k.3).

readOnly.description: tmux-parity `read-only` flag: the client attaches as an observer. CAVEAT (decisions §2.1, verified in tmux source): over control mode, `read-only` does NOT bind the `-CC` command channel — its authority semantics are DRIVER-ENFORCED, never delegated to tmux's flag. The protocol only CARRIES the flag; the driver owns what it means. Reserved slot; no behavior in tc-4b6k.1.
